* outline the key energy sources for exercise;
* distinguish between anaerobic and aerobic sources of energy;
* describe the essential structure of ATP;
* draw and explain the components of the energy continuum;
* describe the role of PCr in ATP synthesis;
* explain how PCr is resynthesized;
* describe the involvement of carbohydrates and fats as energy sources for exercise;
* explain reasons why an athlete is unable to sprint a marathon;
* describe the amounts and sources of energy in the body and their rates of energy formation;
* show how and where the main energy sources are derived and utilized;
* discuss how amino acids can be used as an energy source during exercise.
* elucidate the role of carbohydrates in sport and exercise;
* draw the general structure of a glucose molecule and its isomers;
* draw the general structure of a fructose molecule;
* describe the formation of a disaccharide, illustrating how and where the glycoside bond is present;
* outline the variations in polysaccharides;
* appreciate the structure of glycogen granules;
* describe the processes of glycogenolysis, glycolysis, gluconeogenesis and glycogenesis;
* describe the formation and fates of lactic acid;
* outline the key reactions in the link reaction and the TCA cycle;
* explain the concepts of electron transfer and of oxidative phosphorylation;
* calculate the number of ATP molecules produced during breakdown of a glucose molecule via either the anaerobic and aerobic systems;
* describe the formation of fructose.
* list the key reactions which could potentially contribute to ATP production during intermittent exercise;
* critically evaluate the regulatory processes contributing to energy provision during intermittent exercise;
* critically evaluate the impact of manipulating components of the intermittent protocol (such as duration/intensity of work and rest periods) on regulation of energy provision during exercise;
* critically evaluate the effects of CHO provision on substrate utilization and performance during intermittent exercise;
* critically evaluate the effects of intermittent exercise training on skeletal muscle adaptations depending on the characteristics of the training protocol;
* critically evaluate the effects of intermittent exercise training on metabolic regulation of substrate utilization during sub-maximal exercise;
* critically discuss potential metabolic causes of fatigue during intermittent exercise.
* identify the main chemical elements in the human body;
* describe and draw basic atomic structure;
* identify the differences between atoms, ions, molecules and compounds;
* distinguish the difference between ionic and covalent bonds;
* define a chemical reaction and identify the difference between an endergonic and exergonic reaction;
* define energy and the units of energy;
* describe the role of ATP within the cell;
* describe the common forms of chemical reactions including synthesis, decomposition, reversible, exchange, phosphorylation/dephosphorylation and oxidation/reduction reactions;
* outline the functions of water and the chemical property underpinning water's ability to act as a solvent;
* define the terms mole, molar and molarity;
* define the terms acid, base and salt;
* draw and define the pH scale and describe what is meant by the terms acidic and alkaline;
* describe basic cell structure with particular emphasis to the plasma membrane, nucleus, cytoplasm and mitochondria.

This chapter presents a brief overview of the energy sources used by muscles in order to engage in various activities. It is a 'taster' that will (hopefully) encourage you to delve a bit more deeply into the basic biochemistry of the macronutrients which provide energy, as well as to gain an understanding of the likely regulation of the processes which produce energy. From this perspective, this chapter examines the energy-yielding processes from a superficial level in addressing issues of energy for sprinting and for more prolonged events.

## Definition

Adenosine triphosphate (ATP) is the energy currency of the cell and the only form of energy capable of being used directly for muscle contraction in humans. An ATP molecule consists of a base (adenine) attached to a sugar (ribose), to which three phosphate molecules are attached (see Figure 1.1). The phosphates are attached by 'high energy' bonds which, when removed (i.e., cleaved), release energy that is made available for cellular work.

High-intensity exercise (HIE), in the context of the energy continuum, is equivalent to supra-maximal exercise — i.e., exercise performed at an intensity above maximal aerobic power, where the predominant energy supply is derived from anaerobic processes.

A **chemical reaction** is a process in which substances (reactants) are transformed into different substances (products) via the making and breaking of chemical bonds. Reactions are classified energetically as **exergonic** (net release of free energy to the surroundings) or **endergonic** (net absorption/requirement of free energy from the surroundings). **Energy** is the capacity to do work, expressed in standard units such as the joule (J) and kilojoule (kJ), with the kilocalorie (kcal) also in common biological use.

## Core Principles

- In order for muscles to contract and provide movement, energy is required, and this energy is supplied by ATP.
- ATP is the *only* form of energy capable of being used directly for muscle contraction in humans, making it the obligatory final energy intermediate for all muscular work irrespective of the upstream substrate or pathway.
- The three phosphate groups in ATP are linked by 'high energy' bonds; when one of these bonds is broken (i.e., a phosphate is removed), energy is released and made available for cellular work such as muscle contraction.
- Energy provision for exercise can be conceptualised along an energy continuum, with anaerobic and aerobic sources contributing in different proportions depending on the intensity and duration of activity.
- Phosphocreatine (PCr) plays a central role in rapid ATP synthesis, donating a phosphate group to ADP to regenerate ATP during high-intensity efforts; PCr itself is resynthesized during recovery using ATP derived from aerobic metabolism.
- Carbohydrates and fats serve as the principal substrates for ATP production during exercise, with their relative contributions shifting according to exercise intensity and duration; amino acids can additionally contribute as an energy source during exercise.
- The amounts and sources of energy stored in the body, together with their rates of energy formation, constrain exercise performance and explain why an athlete is unable to sprint a marathon — i.e., the rapid-rate anaerobic systems cannot be sustained over prolonged durations, while aerobic systems cannot match the peak power outputs of sprinting.
- ATP can be regenerated through anaerobic pathways (e.g., glycolysis yielding lactic acid) and aerobic pathways (link reaction, TCA cycle, electron transfer chain and oxidative phosphorylation), with substantially different ATP yields per glucose molecule.
- Carbohydrate metabolism for ATP production proceeds through interlinked processes: glycogenolysis (breakdown of glycogen), glycolysis (breakdown of glucose), gluconeogenesis (synthesis of glucose from non-carbohydrate precursors) and glycogenesis (synthesis of glycogen).
- Carbohydrates occupy a pivotal role in sport and exercise as the substrate that can be metabolised both anaerobically (via glycolysis to lactic acid) and aerobically (via complete oxidation through the TCA cycle and electron transfer chain), making them uniquely versatile across exercise intensities.
- Energy-producing processes must be capable of rapid activation (e.g., transitioning from rest to sprint or explosive activity), sustained appropriate ATP output during prolonged exercise, and rapid inactivation/down-regulation when exercise ceases and muscle energy demand falls.
- The central regulatory question is how the enzymes catalysing energy-producing pathways are activated and inactivated to match ATP supply with the changing demands of rest, exercise and recovery.
- During intermittent exercise, ATP provision draws on multiple reactions in parallel and in sequence across work and rest periods, with the relative contribution of PCr breakdown/resynthesis, glycolysis and aerobic oxidation shifting dynamically as work bouts and recovery intervals alternate.
- Regulation of ATP-producing pathways operates via control of enzyme activity, achieved through hormones (extracellular signals) and intracellular allosteric effectors (e.g., Ca2+, FFAs, lactate, PCr:Cr ratio).
- Manipulating intermittent-protocol components — duration and intensity of work periods and the duration/nature of rest periods — alters the regulation of energy provision by changing substrate depletion/repletion kinetics and the activation state of regulatory enzymes.
- Repeated exercise bouts elicit a training effect by activating transcription and translation factors that enhance synthesis of metabolic enzymes and structural proteins; these factors are themselves driven by intracellular metabolic events (such as increases in Ca2+, FFAs, lactate and changes in the PCr:Cr ratio) and by extracellular signals such as hormones and inflammatory markers.
- Each individual bout of exercise produces enzymatic and structural muscle adaptations, and the cumulative effect of repeated bouts over time constitutes the training effect.
- Intermittent exercise training induces skeletal muscle adaptations whose nature depends on the characteristics of the training protocol, and modifies the metabolic regulation of substrate utilization during sub-maximal exercise.
- Maximal efforts demand the use of anaerobic energy sources together with the recruitment of all muscle fibre types — in particular, the type IIx or fast glycolytic (FG) fibres — so that ATP can be generated in large amounts at a very rapid rate.
- The rate at which ATP is generated determines how much force or velocity can be produced and for how long, linking ATP supply kinetics directly to maximal performance capability.
- Exercise intensity above maximal aerobic power constitutes supra-maximal (high-intensity) exercise, in which the predominant energy supply is derived from anaerobic processes; once significant amounts of energy are drawn from aerobic sources, the activity moves into the domain of prolonged exercise.
- The human body is built from a small set of principal chemical elements, with the chemistry of life dominated by carbon, hydrogen, oxygen and nitrogen, plus important contributions from calcium, phosphorus, potassium, sulphur, sodium, chlorine and magnesium, among others.
- Matter at the biochemical scale is organised hierarchically: **atoms** (a central nucleus of protons and neutrons surrounded by electrons in shells) combine to form **molecules** and **compounds**; atoms that gain or lose electrons become **ions** carrying net positive or negative charge.
- Chemical bonds between atoms are of two principal types relevant to biochemistry: **ionic bonds** (electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions resulting from electron transfer) and **covalent bonds** (sharing of electron pairs between atoms), with covalent bonds dominating the architecture of biological macromolecules.
- Chemical reactions in the cell occur in characteristic forms — **synthesis (anabolic)**, **decomposition (catabolic)**, **reversible**, **exchange**, **phosphorylation/dephosphorylation**, and **oxidation/reduction (redox)** reactions — and ATP-mediated phosphorylation/dephosphorylation together with redox chemistry underpin biological energy transfer.
- Reactions are classified as **exergonic** (release free energy) or **endergonic** (require free energy input); in cells, endergonic processes such as biosynthesis and muscle contraction are typically driven by being coupled to exergonic ATP hydrolysis.
- Energy is defined as the capacity to do work and is measured in joules (J)/kilojoules (kJ), with kilocalories (kcal) used in nutrition; ATP serves as the cell's principal energy-transfer currency, coupling exergonic catabolism to endergonic cellular work.
- Water is the universal biological solvent: its polar covalent O–H bonds confer a permanent dipole, enabling it to solvate ions and polar molecules, participate in hydrolysis/condensation reactions, act as a thermal buffer, and serve as the medium for essentially all intracellular and extracellular biochemistry.
- Solute amounts are quantified using the **mole** (Avogadro's number of particles), with **molar** mass expressed per mole and **molarity** expressed as moles of solute per litre of solution; these units underpin quantitative description of enzyme kinetics, substrate concentrations, and physiological fluids.
- **Acids** donate protons (H+) in solution, **bases** accept protons (or release OH−), and **salts** are the ionic products of acid–base neutralisation; the **pH scale** (0–14, logarithmic) quantifies H+ activity, with pH < 7 acidic, pH = 7 neutral, and pH > 7 alkaline — physiological pH regulation is therefore central to enzyme function and to interpreting exercise-induced acidosis.
- The cell is the fundamental unit of biological organisation; key structural features relevant to energy metabolism include the **plasma membrane** (selectively permeable lipid bilayer controlling exchange of substrates, ions and signals), the **nucleus** (housing DNA and the machinery for transcription that drives training adaptations), the **cytoplasm/cytosol** (site of glycolysis and many regulatory processes), and the **mitochondria** (site of the link reaction, TCA cycle, electron transfer chain and oxidative phosphorylation).

## Sub-concepts

- **Structure of ATP**: adenine (base) + ribose (sugar) + three phosphate groups connected by high-energy bonds (see Figure 1.1). The phosphate groups are connected by 'high energy' bonds whose cleavage releases the energy that powers muscle contraction and other cellular work.
- **ATP as energy currency**: ATP functions as the cellular "energy currency", being the obligatory and sole direct energy donor for muscle contraction in humans; all other energy-yielding processes ultimately serve to regenerate ATP from ADP and Pi. Within the cell, ATP couples exergonic catabolic reactions to endergonic processes such as biosynthesis, active transport and muscle contraction.
- **Energy continuum**: a conceptual framework describing the relative contributions of anaerobic (immediate phosphagen and glycolytic) and aerobic energy systems across the spectrum of exercise intensities and durations.
- **Phosphocreatine (PCr) system**: an immediate, anaerobic source of high-energy phosphate that rapidly regenerates ATP from ADP during explosive/short-duration efforts; PCr is resynthesized during recovery using ATP derived from aerobic metabolism.
- **Substrate sources for exercise**: carbohydrates (muscle and liver glycogen, blood glucose), fats (intramuscular triglyceride, adipose-derived fatty acids) and amino acids, each differing in stored amount, location and rate of energy formation.
- **Monosaccharide structure — glucose**: a six-carbon sugar with a defined general structure that exists in multiple isomeric forms (structural and stereoisomers).
- **Monosaccharide structure — fructose**: a six-carbon ketose sugar with its own characteristic general structure; fructose can be formed via specific metabolic pathways.
- **Disaccharide formation**: two monosaccharide units are joined by a glycoside bond formed at a specific hydroxyl linkage between the sugar units, yielding disaccharides such as sucrose, lactose and maltose.
- **Polysaccharide variations**: polysaccharides differ in their constituent monosaccharide units, chain length, and the type and degree of branching, giving rise to forms such as starch, cellulose and glycogen.
- **Glycogen granule structure**: glycogen is stored as granules with a highly branched architecture that supports rapid mobilisation of glucose units.
- **Carbohydrate metabolic processes**: glycogenolysis (breakdown of glycogen to glucose/glucose-1-phosphate), glycolysis (cytosolic breakdown of glucose to pyruvate/lactate), gluconeogenesis (synthesis of glucose from non-carbohydrate precursors such as lactate, glycerol and amino acids) and glycogenesis (synthesis of glycogen from glucose).
- **Glycolysis and lactic acid**: glucose breakdown via glycolysis can lead to the formation of lactic acid, which has multiple metabolic fates including oxidation back to pyruvate for aerobic use, conversion to glucose via gluconeogenesis (Cori cycle), and shuttling between tissues/fibres.
- **Aerobic oxidation**: the link reaction (pyruvate dehydrogenase converting pyruvate to acetyl-CoA, releasing CO2 and reducing NAD+) and the TCA (Krebs) cycle generate reduced cofactors (NADH, FADH2) that feed into the electron transfer chain, driving oxidative phosphorylation to produce ATP.
- **Electron transfer and oxidative phosphorylation**: electrons from NADH and FADH2 are passed along the inner-mitochondrial-membrane electron transport chain, generating a proton gradient that powers ATP synthase to phosphorylate ADP to ATP, with O2 as terminal electron acceptor.
- **ATP yield from glucose**: anaerobic glycolysis to lactate yields a small net number of ATP per glucose, while complete aerobic oxidation of glucose (glycolysis + link reaction + TCA cycle + oxidative phosphorylation) yields substantially more ATP per glucose molecule.
- **Allosteric effectors**: intracellular chemical compounds (such as Ca2+, FFAs, lactate, and the PCr:Cr ratio) that modulate enzyme activity in response to the metabolic state of the muscle.
- **Hormonal regulation**: extracellular signals impinging on muscle that modulate enzyme activity and gene expression to match energy supply with demand.
- **Transcriptional / translational regulation**: exercise-induced activation of transcription and translation factors that govern the synthesis of metabolic enzymes and structural proteins, underpinning training adaptations; these factors are activated by intracellular metabolic events and by extracellular signals including hormones and inflammatory markers.
- **Intermittent exercise energetics**: the integrated set of reactions (PCr hydrolysis/resynthesis, glycolysis, aerobic oxidation) potentially contributing to ATP supply across alternating work and rest periods.
- **Metabolic fatigue in intermittent exercise**: candidate mechanisms underlying performance decline across repeated work bouts, linked to substrate depletion, accumulation of metabolic by-products, and impaired excitation–contraction coupling.
- **High-intensity / supra-maximal exercise (HIE)**: exercise performed at intensities above maximal aerobic power, where the predominant energy supply is anaerobic. Illustrative example: a well-trained cyclist with VO2max attained at a power output of 400 W and a peak power output (PPO) of 1200 W in a 10-second maximal test (Calbet *et al.*, 2003) reaches VO2max at ≈33% of PPO, leaving ≈67% of PPO as the supra-maximal domain.
- **Fast glycolytic (type IIx / FG) fibres**: muscle fibres recruited maximally during all-out efforts, characterised by a high capacity for rapid anaerobic ATP generation and central to the production of peak force/velocity.
- **Rate of ATP generation**: the determinant of attainable force/velocity output and the duration over which that output can be sustained, providing a kinetic-level link between biochemistry and performance.
- **Chemical elements of the human body**: the body's mass is dominated by a handful of elements — principally carbon, hydrogen, oxygen and nitrogen — with smaller but physiologically critical contributions from calcium, phosphorus, potassium, sulphur, sodium, chlorine and magnesium, and trace contributions from many others.
- **Atomic structure**: a positively charged nucleus of protons and (uncharged) neutrons orbited by negatively charged electrons arranged in shells; chemical behaviour is determined principally by the configuration of outer-shell (valence) electrons.
- **Atoms, ions, molecules, compounds**: an **atom** is the smallest unit of an element; an **ion** is an atom (or group) with net charge from electron gain/loss; a **molecule** is two or more atoms covalently bonded; a **compound** is a substance composed of two or more different elements in fixed proportion.
- **Ionic vs covalent bonding**: **ionic bonds** form by transfer of electrons producing cations and anions held by electrostatic attraction (e.g., NaCl); **covalent bonds** form by sharing of electron pairs (e.g., C–C, C–H, O–H), and may be polar (unequal sharing, as in water) or non-polar.
- **Chemical reaction**: a process converting reactants into products via bond rearrangement; reactions are characterised by stoichiometry, direction (reversible vs effectively irreversible), and energetics (exergonic vs endergonic).
- **Exergonic vs endergonic reactions**: exergonic reactions release free energy and proceed spontaneously; endergonic reactions require free-energy input and in cells are typically driven by coupling to ATP hydrolysis or other exergonic reactions.
- **Energy and its units**: energy is the capacity to do work; SI units are joules (J) and kilojoules (kJ), with kilocalories (kcal) widely used in human nutrition (1 kcal ≈ 4.184 kJ).
- **Forms of chemical reaction**: **synthesis** (combining smaller units into a larger product; anabolic), **decomposition** (breaking a larger molecule into smaller units; catabolic), **reversible** (proceeding in either direction depending on conditions), **exchange** (mutual transfer of atoms/groups between reactants), **phosphorylation/dephosphorylation** (addition/removal of a phosphate group, central to ATP-driven energy transfer and to enzyme regulation), and **oxidation/reduction (redox)** (transfer of electrons, with oxidation = loss and reduction = gain of electrons; underlying electron transport and substrate oxidation).
- **Water as solvent**: water's polar covalent bonds and bent geometry yield a permanent molecular dipole, enabling hydrogen bonding and the hydration/solvation of ions and polar solutes; this underpins water's roles as biological solvent, medium for diffusion and chemical reaction, participant in hydrolysis/condensation, and thermal buffer.
- **Mole, molar, molarity**: a **mole** is 6.022 × 10²³ entities (Avogadro's number); **molar mass** is the mass (g) of one mole of a substance; **molarity (M)** is moles of solute per litre of solution, the standard unit for concentration in biochemistry and physiology.
- **Acids, bases, salts**: an **acid** donates H+ (proton donor), a **base** accepts H+ (or releases OH−), and a **salt** is the ionic compound formed by neutralisation of an acid with a base.
- **pH scale**: a logarithmic scale of H+ activity from 0 to 14; pH < 7 is acidic, pH = 7 is neutral, pH > 7 is alkaline; each unit change represents a tenfold change in H+ concentration, making pH regulation critical to enzyme catalysis and to the interpretation of exercise-induced metabolic acidosis.
- **Cell structure**: the **plasma membrane** is a phospholipid bilayer with embedded proteins that controls selective exchange of substrates, ions and signals; the **nucleus** contains the genome and is the site of transcription underpinning adaptive protein synthesis; the **cytoplasm/cytosol** houses glycolysis, glycogen granules and many regulatory enzymes; the **mitochondria** are the principal site of aerobic ATP production via the link reaction, TCA cycle, electron transport chain and oxidative phosphorylation.

## Practical Applications

- Carbohydrates play a central role in fuelling sport and exercise, supplying substrate for both anaerobic (rapid, short-duration) and aerobic (sustained) ATP production.
- The energy continuum framework guides event-specific fuelling and pacing strategies: sprint/explosive events rely predominantly on PCr and anaerobic glycolysis, whereas endurance events depend on aerobic oxidation of carbohydrates and fats.
- The mismatch between rate and capacity of the different energy systems explains why an athlete cannot sprint a marathon, informing realistic intensity targets across event durations.
- Quantifying ATP yield from anaerobic versus aerobic breakdown of glucose underpins understanding of why glycogen depletion limits high-intensity endurance performance and why carbohydrate availability is critical to sustaining power output.
- Knowledge of glycogenolysis, glycolysis, gluconeogenesis and glycogenesis informs nutritional strategies for glycogen loading, recovery between sessions, and replenishment of liver/muscle glycogen stores.
- Understanding enzyme regulation explains how an athlete can transition rapidly from rest to sprint/explosive activity and sustain ATP production across prolonged exercise, then down-regulate energy production at exercise cessation.
- Repeated training bouts produce enzymatic and structural muscle adaptations through transcription/translation factor activation driven by intracellular metabolic signals (Ca2+, FFAs, lactate, PCr:Cr ratio) and extracellular signals (hormones, inflammatory markers), providing the molecular basis of the training effect.
- Carbohydrate provision before and during intermittent exercise influences substrate utilization and performance, with implications for fuelling strategies in stop-and-go sports.
- Programming of work-to-rest ratios, work intensity and recovery duration in intermittent training can be used to bias adaptations and target specific aspects of metabolic regulation during sub-maximal exercise.
- Identifying the supra-maximal domain for an individual athlete (e.g., as a percentage of PPO above the power eliciting VO2max) supports prescription of HIE training that targets anaerobic energy systems and full recruitment of fast glycolytic (type IIx) fibres.
- Because the rate of ATP generation dictates attainable force/velocity and its sustainability, training and recovery strategies for sprint and power events should prioritise enhancement of rapid ATP-yielding pathways (PCr and anaerobic glycolysis) and recruitment of FG fibres.
- Working fluently with moles, molarity and pH is essential for interpreting blood lactate, glucose and electrolyte data, for preparing experimental and nutritional solutions, and for understanding exercise-induced acidosis and its impact on enzyme function.
- Awareness of water's solvent and thermoregulatory roles underpins hydration strategy: maintaining body water status preserves plasma volume, supports solute transport, and protects the thermal buffering required to sustain prolonged exercise.
- Recognising that mitochondria are the site of aerobic ATP production, while glycolysis occurs in the cytosol, motivates training interventions (e.g., endurance and high-volume work) that increase mitochondrial content and enzymatic capacity to expand aerobic ATP-supply rate.
- Understanding ATP-driven phosphorylation/dephosphorylation and redox chemistry clarifies how exercise and nutritional interventions modulate enzyme activation states and substrate flux through energy-yielding pathways.
